So, another thing I should point out is that we don't necessarily need to use models only as positive evidence.
If you're using log linear interpolation, actually your interpolation coefficients do not need to be positive.
They can also be negative and you can have things where you penalize the probability is given by a particular model.
And this is actually been used for a long time. It was actually used in machine translations since like 2005 or something like this.
So, basic idea is that you have some models that serve as negative evidence.
So, you have kind of a core model. This might be a really strong general purpose language model.
You have a positive model, which is the model that you want to kind of boost up and improve in a negative model, which you want to decrease.
One example of this is in a paper that we did in 2019.
The core was a machine translation model.
And the negative model is an out of domain language model.
And the positive model is an end domain language model.
And so the idea behind this is a machine translation model.
You have to train it on machine translation data and machine translation data is not very easy to get for particular domains, for example.
You might only have machine translation data in the news domain and you actually want to be doing translation in the medical domain or something.
So, what you do is you have your positive model here. This could be a new, this is a machine translation model.
This could be a news domain or sorry, this could be a medical domain language model.
And this could be a news domain language model. So, you're subtracting out the news domain probabilities and adding a medical domain probabilities.
Another example of this is something called the experts or dexperts.
And the idea here is here you have a strong language model is your core.
And then as negative you have a weak toxic language model.
So, it was trained on lots of like bad text that you don't want to be generating.
And the positive is a weak non-taxic language model that was trained on lots of like innocuous posts.
So, that would help you de-taxify the outputs of the language model.
So, there's lots of examples of things like this that we can do.

So, another thing that I didn't actually put it on the slides is there's a lot of other ways to get multiple models.
And I think a lot of people are probably familiar with dropout.
It's a method for regularizing.
It's a method for regularizing neural networks of deep learning models in general.
The idea is every once in a while.
During training, you drop out some portion of the, like nodes in the neural network model.
And you can actually drop out.
And normally what you do is it test time, then you just don't drop out anything and you use a whole neural network model.
But another thing you can do is you can drop out at test time, drop out five times in combined those different models together through ensemble.
And that's actually something that people tried in the, in the dropout paper.
